useful_fact_handles.why: The group order denominator appears in the probability budget.
The query-bound numerator appears in the probability budget.
Product-bound side conditions often need non-negativity of factors.
Ratio factors in the bound usually need numerator/denominator non-negativity.
Powered ratio factors often need exponent non-negativity closure.
Strict positivity is useful when rewriting ratio-over-itself side conditions.
Strictly positive denominator/numerator facts support positive ratio factors.
Powered positive ratio factors stay positive.
B/B residuals often close by rewriting `divrr` after proving B is nonzero.
Membership-event probability bounds often use list-size or union-bound style lemmas when available.

side_condition_recipe.name: B_over_B residual
side_condition_recipe.read_as: When a residual becomes a budget divided by the same budget, treat it as B/B: establish B <> 0 or B > 0, then rewrite with `divrr`.

facts_and_diagnostics.fact_note: Only current-frontier facts are shown here; the full proof context artifact keeps the compiler/internal details.

unsafe_lowerings.reason: A single accepted `rnd` step, or a compact `do N!rnd`, may leave a subgoal that charges local samples against the entire product budget.
EasyCrypt may accept `phoare split !`, but the residual goals can still be a bad route under a product budget.
A plausible `seq K : side_fact` can move the small product budget onto a high-probability prefix or deterministic side condition.
Direct `call (_: true).` under a one-sided `[<=]` bound can fail because EasyCrypt expects a compatible bound direction.

budget_ledger.read_as: A probability budget ledger: factor slots are the resources in the product bound, and event obligations are the proof events that should spend those resources before local sampling cleanup.

likely_proof_family: split the event/budget before descending into procedure proof

useful_fact_handles.why: The group order denominator appears in the probability budget.
The query-bound numerator appears in the probability budget.
Product-bound side conditions often need non-negativity of factors.
Ratio factors in the bound usually need numerator/denominator non-negativity.
Powered ratio factors often need exponent non-negativity closure.
Strict positivity is useful when rewriting ratio-over-itself side conditions.
Strictly positive denominator/numerator facts support positive ratio factors.
Powered positive ratio factors stay positive.
B/B residuals often close by rewriting `divrr` after proving B is nonzero.
Membership-event probability bounds often use list-size or union-bound style lemmas when available.

side_condition_recipe.name: B_over_B residual
side_condition_recipe.read_as: When a residual becomes a budget divided by the same budget, treat it as B/B: establish B <> 0 or B > 0, then rewrite with `divrr`.

anti_routes.reason: A single accepted `rnd` step, or a compact `do N!rnd`, may leave a subgoal that charges local samples against the entire product budget.
EasyCrypt may accept `phoare split !`, but the residual goals can still be a bad route under a product budget.
A plausible `seq K : side_fact` can move the small product budget onto a high-probability prefix or deterministic side condition.
Direct `call (_: true).` under a one-sided `[<=]` bound can fail because EasyCrypt expects a compatible bound direction.

probability_budget.read_as: Advisory middle-end context for route selection; this is not a proof script or tactic order.

notes.message: One-sided sampling residual: G1.x{1} <$ dt. The postcondition still mentions the sampled value or a quantified witness, so this may be a true losslessness side condition or leftover coupling debt.

evidence: recent accepted/probed local sampling under a product probability budget
recent accepted/probed `seq` cut may allocate the product budget to the wrong branch
route memory contains previously bad probability-budget tactic shapes

items: current evidence points at a structural recovery boundary

related_checkpoints.label: Before product-budget seq cut #6
related_checkpoints.why_checkpoint: Revisit this product-budget seq cut; the current probability budget residual may need a stronger midpoint assertion or a different budget allocation.

related_checkpoints.label: Before product-budget seq cut #6
related_checkpoints.why_checkpoint: seq-cut boundary; selecting it restores the proof state before this cut was committed

related_checkpoints.label: Before call invariant #7
related_checkpoints.why_checkpoint: seq-local branch boundary; selecting it keeps the seq cut and removes branch-local work after it

related_checkpoints.label: After call opened / before obligation work #8
related_checkpoints.why_checkpoint: call-local boundary; selecting it keeps the call tactic and removes work committed after the call opened

limitations: does not infer a replacement invariant, cut formula, or postcondition
uses only current residual evidence and recent local repair failures

inspect_lookup_handles.effect: All handles here are read-only manager requests; the committed EasyCrypt proof state stays unchanged.
inspect_lookup_handles.manager_note: Some requests may ask EasyCrypt or name resolution; wait for the manager result before choosing the next proof intent.

ask_manager_for.use_when: Need parsed goal shape, resolved names, or KB pattern hints.
The current procedure frontier exposes a call route.
ask_manager_for.returns: call-ready/oracle-equiv context for the current frontier, plus the BODY of the procedure(s) called here (functor aliases resolved) so you can see which oracles/state your call invariant must preserve

ask_manager_for.use_when: You are considering `call (_: Inv)` and already have a concrete invariant expression you want to test first.
ask_manager_for.returns: a read-only preview of the obligations caused by that candidate invariant, including pre/init, call-preservation, oracle-side, postcondition, missing-fact signals, and extra conjuncts that may create avoidable obligations

ask_manager_for.use_when: Need the valid form for call, while, seq, rnd, or rewrite.
ask_manager_for.returns: valid tactic forms and common traps

ask_manager_for.use_when: Need the valid one-sided hoare/phoare loop form.
Mid-proof pRHL suffix surgery may need indexed `wp`.
ask_manager_for.returns: valid indexed `wp` forms and suffix-alignment traps

ask_manager_for.use_when: Statement order may need a small `swap` before `sp`, `wp`, or `sim`.
A guarded branch may need `rcondt` after a case split or invariant fact.
A guarded branch may need `rcondf` after a case split or invariant fact.
A suffix proof may need `conseq` to weaken to a smaller relation before `sim`.
ask_manager_for.returns: valid `conseq` forms and pRHL memory-annotation traps

ask_manager_for.use_when: One side may have an extra sample or need an explicit sample coupling.
A known statement-order mismatch may need an eager/lazy transformation.
ask_manager_for.returns: valid `eager` forms and when to prefer smaller surgery

items.label: Before product-budget seq cut #6
items.committed_tactic: seq 13 : (size G3.cilog <= size G1.log /\ size G1.log <= PKE_.qD).
items.why_checkpoint: seq-cut boundary; selecting it restores the proof state before this cut was committed
items.effect_if_selected: Undo committed tactic #6 and every committed tactic after it in this node.
